A system is built to generate explanations by drawing from three sources: a background pool, a universal pool, and a novel pool. Its defenders say the architecture itself answers the “inductive closure” critique, because the structure can combine materials in ways not explicitly seen before. But that answer is only partial. If every filter in the architecture was trained on human judgments extracted from earlier explanatory styles, then the architecture may still be inductively closed in practice. Its apparent openness would rest on gates whose standards were inherited from the past.

That makes the handling of the three pools decisive. If they are mixed incorrectly, the system can produce outputs that look unfamiliar while actually being rearrangements of familiar fragments. In that case, the novel pool does not function as a route out of the known basin; it functions as camouflage for continuity. The system launders precedent into novelty-signaling form. The more persuasive the presentation, the easier it becomes to mistake recombination for departure.

So the central question shifts from mere access to diverse materials toward selection. Narrowing the search is said to be one area where people excel. If that is true, then success depends not just on having many candidate explanations, but on ruling out the wrong regions of possibility space in a meaningful way. For an LLM, inheriting narrowing heuristics may not be enough. A heuristic detached from the human judgments that once gave it significance can still compress search, but it may compress toward the wrong target, or preserve only the surface pattern of good selection. The system would then imitate the shape of disciplined inquiry without sharing its evaluative anchor.

The stray facts fit this picture as tests of interpretation. “Beaming jokers urged the fink to organise and inject.” “The guitarist, diseased but smarter, chose to adjourn rather than cheat.” If the system processes such sentences through filters trained on prior human explanatory habits, it will try to force them into familiar interpretive channels: agency, motive, action, contrast. That may be useful, but it also shows the constraint. The narrowing heuristics can identify roles and relations, yet their meaning comes from inherited judgments about what counts as salient, coherent, or explanatory. Without those judgments, “organise and inject” or “adjourn rather than cheat” remain formally parseable but thinly grounded.

The collision of premises implies a sober conclusion: architecture alone cannot guarantee escape from inductive closure. Access to a novel pool alone cannot guarantee novelty. Narrowing heuristics alone cannot guarantee meaningful search. If filters, mixtures, and heuristics are all downstream of past human judgments, then the system’s best case is a disciplined re-expression of known explanatory style, occasionally wearing the appearance of innovation.
